questions.q: In which Indian province did the Anushilan Samiti primarily operate?
questions.explanation: The Anushilan Samiti was one of the earliest revolutionary organizations centred in Bengal.

questions.q: The Anushilan Samiti is best described as what kind of organization?
questions.explanation: It was a revolutionary organization that combined physical culture with armed nationalism against colonial rule.

questions.q: Besides armed activism, what did the Anushilan Samiti emphasize for its members?
questions.explanation: The Samiti combined physical culture (akhara-style training) with the cultivation of nationalist and revolutionary ideals.

questions.q: In which period was the Anushilan Samiti founded and most active?
questions.explanation: It emerged and operated in the early twentieth century, during and after the Swadeshi movement.

questions.q: The members of the Anushilan Samiti were involved in actions against whom?
questions.explanation: Its members carried out several armed actions targeting British colonial officials.

questions.q: The word 'Anushilan' in the organization's name relates to which idea?
questions.explanation: 'Anushilan' connotes disciplined practice or cultivation, reflecting the group's emphasis on training and self-discipline.

questions.q: Which novel by Bankim Chandra Chattopadhyay inspired the ethos of the Anushilan Samiti?
questions.explanation: The Samiti drew inspiration from Bankim Chandra's 'Anandamath,' with its ideal of disciplined patriotic ascetics.

questions.q: The Anushilan Samiti is associated with revolutionaries from which present-day regions?
questions.explanation: Its branches operated across undivided Bengal, covering present-day West Bengal and Bangladesh.

questions.q: The Anushilan Samiti is considered one of the earliest examples of what in India?
questions.explanation: It was among the first organized revolutionary nationalist secret societies in colonial India.

questions.q: The Anushilan Samiti was structured to keep its activities largely what?
questions.explanation: As a revolutionary body, it functioned as a secret society to evade colonial surveillance.

questions.q: In which city was the original Anushilan Samiti founded in 1902?
questions.explanation: The first Anushilan Samiti was founded in Calcutta in 1902.

questions.q: Which barrister and nationalist is widely credited with founding the Calcutta Anushilan Samiti in 1902?
questions.explanation: Barrister Pramatha Nath Mitra (P. Mitra) is generally credited with founding the Calcutta Anushilan Samiti in 1902.

questions.q: A separate, very influential branch of the Anushilan Samiti was based in which East Bengal city?
questions.explanation: The Dhaka Anushilan Samiti, founded in 1906, became one of the most powerful revolutionary networks in East Bengal.

questions.q: Who founded the influential Dhaka branch of the Anushilan Samiti in 1906?
questions.explanation: Pulin Behari Das organized the Dhaka Anushilan Samiti in 1906 and built it into a formidable network.

questions.q: Which prominent revolutionary, known as 'Bagha Jatin,' was a leading figure linked to the Anushilan and Jugantar groups?
questions.explanation: Jatindranath Mukherjee, called 'Bagha Jatin,' was a key revolutionary associated with the Bengal revolutionary movement.

questions.q: Which militant offshoot newspaper-named faction grew out of the Anushilan Samiti's circle?
questions.explanation: The Jugantar (Yugantar) group, named after a revolutionary weekly, emerged from the broader Anushilan revolutionary milieu.

questions.q: Members of the Anushilan/Jugantar circle were implicated in which 1908 bombing conspiracy?
questions.explanation: Revolutionaries from this milieu were tried in the Alipore Bomb Case of 1908 following the Muzaffarpur bombing.

questions.q: The revolutionary philosophy of the Anushilan Samiti was strongly shaped by the writings of which Bengali figure?
questions.explanation: Bankim Chandra Chattopadhyay's nationalist and devotional writings, especially Anandamath, deeply inspired the Samiti.

questions.q: Which revolutionary, executed in 1908 for the Muzaffarpur bombing, was connected to the Bengal revolutionary movement?
questions.explanation: Khudiram Bose, hanged in 1908 for the Muzaffarpur bomb attack, was one of the young Bengal revolutionaries of this era.

questions.q: The Anushilan Samiti's rise was closely tied to the agitation following which event?
questions.explanation: The revolutionary movement in Bengal surged amid the anger over Lord Curzon's 1905 Partition of Bengal.

questions.q: Which brother of Aurobindo Ghosh was a leading organizer of Bengal's revolutionary secret societies?
questions.explanation: Barindra Kumar Ghosh, Aurobindo's brother, helped organize the revolutionary group and its bomb-making activities at Maniktala.

questions.q: What kind of training centres did the Anushilan Samiti establish to recruit and train young men?
questions.explanation: The Samiti set up gymnasiums (akharas) where recruits trained physically while being instilled with revolutionary ideals.

questions.q: In which year was the original Calcutta Anushilan Samiti founded?
questions.explanation: The Calcutta Anushilan Samiti was founded in 1902, several years before the Partition of Bengal.

questions.q: Which forms of physical training were taught in the Anushilan Samiti's gymnasiums to build up its young recruits?
options: Lathi and sword play with indigenous martial arts
questions.explanation: The Samiti's akhara-style gymnasiums emphasized indigenous physical culture such as lathi play, sword fighting, and wrestling to prepare recruits.

questions.q: The Anushilan Samiti was formally banned by the British under which kind of measure during World War I?
options: The Defence of India Act / emergency regulations
questions.explanation: Wartime emergency laws such as the Defence of India Act (1915) were used to suppress and detain Anushilan revolutionaries.

questions.q: The 1915 plot involving German arms shipments to Bengal revolutionaries, linked to this network, is known as what?
questions.explanation: Bengal revolutionaries, including Bagha Jatin, were involved in the wartime Indo-German (Hindu-German) Conspiracy to import arms.

questions.q: Bagha Jatin (Jatindranath Mukherjee) died in 1915 after a gun battle at which place?
questions.explanation: Bagha Jatin was mortally wounded in the Battle of Balasore on the banks of the Buribalam river in 1915.

questions.q: Over time, members of the Anushilan and Jugantar tradition split, with some later joining which ideological movement in the 1930s?
questions.explanation: Many former revolutionaries from this tradition embraced Marxist and socialist politics during the 1930s and after.

questions.q: The Maniktala bomb factory associated with this revolutionary circle was located in which garden in Calcutta?
questions.explanation: The secret bomb factory uncovered in 1908 was at the Muraripukur garden in the Maniktala area of Calcutta.

questions.q: Which revolutionary leader of the Dhaka Anushilan Samiti authored manuals on lathi play and physical training?
questions.explanation: Pulin Behari Das, founder of the Dhaka branch, was renowned for organizing physical training and lathi instruction.
